coredata.dc:title: Hypochlorite responsive ratiometric fluorescent switch and logic gates based on lanthanide functionalized polymer nanosphere
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Zhan
Wu, Haixia
Li, Feifei
Ma, Lufang
Qiao, Xiaoguang
coredata.dc:description: A novel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe (Eu(NTA)3L@PS) based on lanthanide functionalized polymer nanosphere has been designed and prepared through a typical miniemulsion polymerization process. Eu(NTA)3L@PS displays a strong red emission in aqueous environment due to the protection of PS layer. The red fluorescence (λem = 618 nm) will gradually quench and the green emission (λem = 496 nm) increase toward ClO− in the presence of H+. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F618/F496) can accurately monitor ClO− concentration with the low limit of detection (57.3 nM). Moreover, Eu(NTA)3L@PS can be used as an optical logic device for “AND” logic gate with ClO− and H+ as two inputs, red and green emission as two outputs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: lanthanide
Logic gate
Ratiometric fluorescent
Hypochlorite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Miniemulsion polymerization of Eu(NTA)3L@PS and L@PS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, (B) SEM image and (C、D、E) elemental mapping images of Eu(NTA)3L@PS. Inset: photographs of the Eu(NTA)3L@PS emulsion under daylight (left) and exposure to UV excitation at 365 nm (right).

(A) Fluorescence intensity response of Eu(NTA)3L@PS with increasing concentration of ClO− (0–20 μM) in the presence of 3 eq. H+. Inset: ClO− concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity ratio F618/F496 decreased of Eu(NTA)3L@PS. (B) Fluorescent intensity ratio (F618/F496) of Eu(NTA)3L@PS toward 100 μM various analytes in aqueous solution. (1) only probe; (2) Ca2+; (3) Zn2+; (4) Mg2+; (5) Cu2+; (6) Ni2+; (7) Fe2+; (8) Mn2+; (9) Cd2+; (10) Hg2+; (11) Al3+; (12) Fe3+; (13) PO4 3−; (14) SO4 2−; (15) NO3 −; (16) NO2 −; (17) MnO4 2−; (18) MnO4 −; (19) Cr2Q7 2−; (20) ClO−.

(A) Emission spectra of L in the presence various concentrations H+ (0–3 eq.) (B). Fluorescence emission spectra of L@PS (pink line), L@PS + H+ (black line), L@PS + ClO− (blue line) and L@PS + H+ + ClO− (green line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Column diagram of fluorescence intensities of FEu and FL: the black dashed lines show the thresholds (0.2), (B) truth table and (C) electronic equivalent circuitry of the Boolean logic gates containing two input signals.

(A) Column diagram of normalized fluorescence intensities of FEu and FL: the black dashed lines show the thresholds (FEu: 0.5; FL: 0.2) toward 3 eq. H+ and various concentrations of ClO− (0, 6, 10, 14 μM). (B) Truth table of the logic analytical device for ClO− monitoring.
Schematic illustration of Eu(NTA)3L@PS nanosphere for the detection of ClO− and its logic gate performance.

head.ce:title: Hypochlorite responsive ratiometric fluorescent switch and logic gates based on lanthanide functionalized polymer nanosphere

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Henan Key Laboratory of Function-Oriented Porous Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe (Eu(NTA)3L@PS) based on lanthanide functionalized polymer nanosphere has been designed and prepared through a typical miniemulsion polymerization process. Eu(NTA)3L@PS displays a strong red emission in aqueous environment due to the protection of PS layer. The red fluorescence (λem = 618 nm) will gradually quench and the green emission (λem = 496 nm) increase toward ClO− in the presence of H+. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F618/F496) can accurately monitor ClO− concentration with the low limit of detection (57.3 nM). Moreover, Eu(NTA)3L@PS can be used as an optical logic device for “AND” logic gate with ClO− and H+ as two inputs, red and green emission as two outputs.

Eu(NTA)3L@PS with uniform microscopic morphology, high photothermal stability, and strong red emission could be used as optical probe toward ClO− in the presence of H+. Taking H+ and ClO− as input signals, red and green emission band as output signals, two-input and two-output logic gate has been developed and presented well switching ratio.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Plenty of luminescence probes have been developed with rare earth coordination compounds owing to their special fluorescence behavior including long lifetime, narrow emission peak, high sensitivity, large stoke shift, high fluorescence quantum yield and so on [1–12]. However, the direct application of small organic molecule rare earth complexes was constricted as they were always light and thermal unstable. Therefore, various of materials were employed to protect the small organic molecule rare earth complexes and extended wide variety of rare earth-based luminescence hybrid materials [13–23]. For example, rigid inorganic hosts like silica or titanium dioxide were employed to protect the emissive centers, and significantly increased the decomposition temperature of the complex. The morphology controllability of the hosts provides more possibility of the multi-functional probes. One-dimensional rod-like, two-dimensional planar materials, and mesoporous structures may exhibit better sensing properties in comparison with nanoparticles due to the larger surface area [24–31]. Another strategy was the incorporation of luminescence complex with polymers and soft materials, which can increase the processability and flexibility of probes [32–38]. Moreover, the properties of the protect materials provided specific response to some guest molecules. Very recently, our group reported the preparation of organosilica core and silica shell double protected lanthanide complexes, through a biphasic sol-gel process and a Pickering emulsion process [39]. Compared with the free rare earth complexes, which is response to many ions, the double protected probe has a specific ClO− detection with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Notably, the selective responsibility of rare earth-based luminescence complex has been considered to have promising prospect in the field of molecule logic gates [40–42]. One can easily extend the mutations of narrow-band emission spectra and characteristic color changes observed by naked eyes to basic ‘‘0″ and ‘‘1″ states for logic systems of binary. Compared to some well-designed small organic molecule rare earth complexes, hybrids seem to have the broader prospects for development according to the advantages as mentioned above [43,44]. Recently, the logic gate performance of lanthanide-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), silicon quantum dots, gold nanoparticles, supermolecules have been reported [45–47]. However, few reports can be found for the combination of luminescence complex and common polymers such as polystyrene (PS) and poly methyl methacrylate (PMMA), which are widely used in coating, printing and film formation industry.

Compared to classical detection methods at a single wavelength, ratiometric fluorescent chemosensors can exhibit the delicate ariation of emission bands upon binding with analytes, owing to its advantages on avoiding errors associated with receptor concentration, photobleaching, and environmental effects. In this work, we reported the preparation of polymer matrix (PS) based ratiometric fluorescent probe (Eu(NTA)3L@PS) and construction of an “AND” logic system with inputs of H+ and ClO−. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , we designed and synthesized europium complex (Eu(NTA)3L) which has strong red emission under UV irradiation. Through a typical miniemulsion polymerization process, the Eu(NTA)3L probes were then encapsulated in the PS colloidal latex particles. Owing to the PS protect layer, H+ cannot reach the Eu(NTA)3L probes if no ClO− is existed. In contrary, when the ClO− open the gate, the Eu(NTA)3L probes will be destroyed by H+ and present green emission under UV irradiation.

4-Bromobenzaldehyde (98%), 4-Vinylphenylbronic acid (98%), 2-Acetylpyridine (98%), EuCl3·6H2O (99.9%), 4,4,4-trifluoro-1-(naphthalen-2-yl)butane-1,3-dione (NTA, 97%), Styrene (St, 99.5%), Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%) and 2,2′-Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All the other chemicals were analytical grade reagents and used as received. All solvents and other metal salts of the highest purity available were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd.

1H NMR spectra and high-resolution mass spectrometry (HR-MS) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance 600 MHz NMR Spectrometer. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was measured using a Tescan 5136 MM scanning electron microscope. Elemental analyses were collected by Thermo Electron Flash EA112. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on TECHCOMP spectrophotometer in the range of 200–500 nm with a slit of 2 nm. Fluorescence and excitation spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 150 W xenon lamp as a light source. All the experiments were performed at ambient temperature.

The Eu(NTA)3L complex was prepared by mixing ligand L (0.5 mmol, 206 mg) and Eu(NTA)3 (0.5 mmol, 450 mg) in ethanol. After the mixture was heated to 90 °C and refluxed for 3 h. The solvent was partially removed under reduced pressure and the resulting material was dried at 65 °C in a vacuum oven overnight to give the solid complex (Fig. 1 ). Elemental analysis was carried out for Eu(NTA)3L (found (%): C, 62.70; H, 3.81; Eu, 17.98; F, 6.70; N, 5.02; O, 3.79. Calc. (%): C, 62.79; H, 3.59; Eu, 18.05; F, 6.77; N, 4.99; O, 3.80).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Miniemulsion polymerization of Eu(NTA)3L@PS and L@PS
ce:section.ce:para: Eu(NTA)3L (0.025 mmol, 12 mg) was first dissolved in 200 μL dichloromethane and mixed with St (8 mol, 832 mg). AIBN (0.1 mol, 16.4 mg) was then added, and the mixture was poured in 10 mL aqueous solution containing CTAB (0.1 mol, 36.4 mg). After probe sonication for 2 min (500 W), the miniemulsion was purged with nitrogen for 30 min and placed in a preheated oil bath at 65 °C for overnight. The polymerization was quenched by cooling down the reaction mixture to room temperature and obtain Eu(NTA)3L@PS (Fig. 1). L@PS was synthesized in the similar procedure as Eu(NTA)3L@PS, except for the use of L (0.025 mmol).

Ligand L with a polymerizable vinyl group was first synthesized based on a classic Suzuki coupling reaction and Kröhnke cyclization reaction (Fig. S1), and then coordinate to Eu(NTA)3 to produce complex Eu(NTA)3L (Fig. 1). Eu(NTA)3L-base polymer hybrids colloidal particles were then prepared through a typical miniemulsion polymerization process, with St as the monomer, CTAB as the surfactant and AIBN as the initiator. Owing to the characteristic of miniemulsion polymerization, hydrophobic Eu(NTA)3L was encapsulated by the monomer droplets in the beginning of the reaction. In addition, the vinyl group in the L can be involved in the radical polymerization reaction and induced the anchoring of luminescence groups in the polymer chains and latex particles. Fig. 2 A and B shows the TEM and SEM images of final Eu(NTA)3L@PS hybrid particles, in which monodispersed particles with a size of 100 nm can be observed. The encapsulation of Eu(NTA)3L in the latex particles was confirmed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis as shown in Fig. 2C, D and 2E, in which the typical elements of N, O and Eu were found uniformly distributed in the particles.

TGA analysis was employed to evaluate the positive effect of polymer matrix on enhancing thermal stability of the complex. As shown in Fig. S5, the weight loss of the Eu(NTA)3L complex and Eu(NTA)3L@PS hybrid particles were determined from the residual weight at 700 °C. The degradation temperature for the Eu(NTA)3L complex appeared at 180 °C, owing to the cleavage of coordination structure and decomposition of organic components. But for the PS protected Eu(NTA)3L, the degradation temperature shifted to 300 °C, showing that the thermal stability of the hybrid particles was improved. The reason can be explained as that the change of the coordination structure was limited by the PS chains which may also build a barrier for the release of the degradation products.

Fluorescence titration was applied to investigate the responsiveness of the Eu(NTA)3L@PS probe (0.05 wt%) to the ClO− in this work. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the probe exhibited a strong red emission at 618 nm without ClO−, and then quickly decreased with the increase of ClO− content (0–20 μM) in the present of 3 eq H+. Simultaneously, the slowly increase of FL intensity at 496 nm can be observed, showing the characteristic of ratiometric fluorescent. The emission of the probe solution finally switched from red to green as shown in the photo (right inset of Fig. 3A). The fluorescence intensity ratio (F618/F496) is weakened linearly with the increase of ClO− concentration from 0 to 14 μM. The linear equation could be represented by Y = 9.303–0.664 X (left inset of Fig. 3A). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 57.3 nM according to the equation LOD = 3SD/slope, where SD is the standard deviation for the fluorescent intensity of HPs in the absence of ClO−. Parallel experiments were carried out for 5 times and the relative standard deviation was calculated to be 1.27%. In comparison with previous reported fluorescent probes, the LOD in this research (57.3 nM) was quite low (Table S1).

The selectivity response of this ratiometric fluorescent on ClO− detection was investigated in the comparison with other interferents including Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, NO2 −, MnO4 2−, MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−. As shown in Fig. 3B, all the interferences had little effect on the ratiometric fluorescent of the probe except for ClO−, showing that the Eu(NTA)3L@PS probe exhibited specific ClO− detection with high sensitivity and quick response. But it is also should be noted here the addition of ClO− can change the fluorescence color from red to green only in the presence of H+.

In order to investigate the mechanism of specific detection of ClO− with the Eu(NTA)3L@PS probe, fluorescence measurement and UV titration were performed for ligand L. The optimal excitation wavelength for ligand L is 303 nm (Fig. S6). Fig. 4 A shows the FL emission spectra of ligand L with different concentration of H+. The FL emission intensity keep decreasing with the increase of H+, and the emission peak slowly shifted from 420 nm to 500 nm. And the FL intensity at 500 nm increased with the continuous increase of H+ concentration. However, the position of the emission peaks changed little for the L@PS probe with H+, in comparison with pure L@PS probe as shown in Fig. 4B. The same FL behavior for L@PS probe with only ClO− was also observed. The shift of emission peak in the present of both H+ and ClO− illustrated that the synergy of the two elements is the key for specific ClO− detection with PS protected probes. As it has been proved somewhere else [39], the polymer shell of the probe can be swelled by ClO−, and allowed the access of H+ which then caused the variation of FL emission. UV–vis spectra shows the ultraviolet absorption peak of ligand L changed from the initial peak (at 301 nm) to two peaks (at 242 nm and 332 nm respectively) with the increase of H+ concentration, and the absorption intensity gradually weakened and showed a large left shift. It thus can be deduced that the pyridine nitrogen atoms in the ligand L was protonated by H+, and results in different π-π* of the ligand L (Fig. S7).

Based on the collaborative response of H+ and ClO−, Eu(NTA)3L@PS probe was then able to be developed as a typical two-input logic gate for the monitoring of ClO−. The logic gate mode was established by using 10 μM ClO− and 3 eq. H+ as the inputs, the presence and absence of ClO− and H+ were defined as “1” and “0” respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence signals of Ligand L (FL) and Eu(NTA)3L (FEu) were taken as the output, for which the emission output values larger and less than 0.2 were defined as binary “1” and “0”. The column bars of the emission values and the truth table for the decoder logic gate are given in Fig. 5B, showing the different responses of the Eu(NTA)3L@PS under various input states. The output of FEu is composed of an “AND” gate and a “NOT” gate (small circle), for which the gate can be activated and exerted an output signal of “1” when the system received no input (0/0) or one input (0/1 and 1/0). The output “AND” gate for FL can only be activated when the system received two inputs together (1/1), and exerted an output signal of “1” (Fig. 5C).

According to the logic operation, direct detection for ClO− concentration can be expected. As shown in Fig. 6 A, ClO− with four concentrations from 0 to 14 μM were set as the input signals in this work. The output signals were normalized as follow: the emission output values larger and less than 0.5 were defined as binary “1” and “0” for the output FEu, and larger and less than 0.2 were defined as binary “1” and “0” for the output FL. As shown in Fig. 6B, the output “AND” gate and “NOT” gate for FEu were activated when the concentration of ClO− below 10.0 μM, and the output “AND” gate for FL was triggered by 14.0 μM of ClO−. Therefore, simple binary codes can be generated as the low and middle concentrations of ClO− were reflected by the outputs (0/1 and 0/0), and the outputs (1, 0) encode the high concentrations of ClO−. Moreover, each concentration has a standard color that can be observed. Three groups were separated with their representative colors: 0–6 (red), 6–10 (yellow) and 10–14 (green). Therefore, the ClO− concentration can be analyzed via color discrimination according to this designed logic device.

In summary, a typical miniemulsion polymerization method has been used for preparation of the homogeneous lanthanide complex encapsulated ratiometric fluorescent polymer nanosphere. The obtained Eu(NTA)3L@PS with uniform microscopic morphology, high photothermal stability, and strong red emission could be used as an optical probe towards ClO− in the presence of H+. Much enhanced specific detection of ClO− have been achieved on Eu(NTA)3L@PS as compared to the free Eu(NTA)3L due to its PS protect layer. Significantly, taking H+ and ClO− as input signals, red and green emission band as output signals, two-input and two-output logic gate has been developed and presented well switching ratio. This work paves a way to rationally assemble lanthanide complex based polymer, and combine their advantages to realize molecular monitoring.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Key Scientific Research Project of Higher Education of Henan Province (No. 17A150015), the Technology Innovation Team Support Programs of Henan Province (16A150016 and 172102210098), the Postdoc research grand in Henan Province (No. 1901007) and the Project of Central Plains Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talents, Henan Province (to Lufang Ma).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.108033.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multimetallic lanthanide complexes: using kinetic control to define complex multimetallic arrays

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.J. Sorensen, S. Faulkner, Multimetallic lanthanide complexes: using kinetic control to define complex multimetallic arrays, Acc. Chem. Res. 51 (2018) 2493-2501.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards polymetallic lanthanide complexes as dual contrast agents for magnetic resonance and optical imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Debroye, T.N. Parac-Vogt, Towards polymetallic lanthanide complexes as dual contrast agents for magnetic resonance and optical imaging, Chem. Soc. Rev. 43 (2014) 8178-8192.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prevailing paradigms in novel lanthanide optical probes from molecular complexes to hybrid materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zheng, C.C. Zhang, Q. Wang, Prevailing paradigms in novel lanthanide optical probes from molecular complexes to hybrid materials, Sensors Actuat. B-Chem. 245 (2017) 622-640.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An optical anion chemosensor based on a europium complex and its molecular logic behavior

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, B. Li, L. Zhang, P. Li, H. Jiang, An optical anion chemosensor based on a europium complex and its molecular logic behavior, Dyes Pigm. 97 (2013) 26-31.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in the field of supramolecular lanthanide luminescent sensors and self-assemblies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Dossantos, A. Harte, S. Quinn, T. Gunnlaugsson, Recent developments in the field of supramolecular lanthanide luminescent sensors and self-assemblies, Coordin. Chem. Rev. 252 (2008) 2512-2527.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescence modulation of two individual fluorophores over a wide pH range and intracellular studies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhou, C.C. Zhang, Y. Zheng, Q. Wang, Luminescence modulation of two individual fluorophores over a wide pH range and intracellular studies, Dyes Pigm. 150 (2018) 151-157.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective visualization of endogenous hypochlorous acid in zebrafish during lipopolysaccharide-induced acute liver injury using a polymer micelles-based ratiometric fluorescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Zhang, H. Wang, Y. Hong, M. Yu, R. Zeng, Y. Long, J. Chen, Selective visualization of endogenous hypochlorous acid in zebrafish during lipopolysaccharide-induced acute liver injury using a polymer micelles-based ratiometric fluorescent probe, Biosens. Bioelectro. 99 (2018) 318-324.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Europium functionalized silicon quantum dots nanomaterials for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Bacillus anthrax biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhou, J. Gu, Y. Chen, X. Zhang, H. Wu, X. Qiao, Europium functionalized silicon quantum dots nanomaterials for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Bacillus anthrax biomarker, Spectrochim. Acta A 212 (2019) 88-93.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent magnetic nanoparticles encapsulated in MOFs for highly selective and sensitive detection of ClO-/SCN- and anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, J. Hai, S. Li, B. Wang, Z. Yang, Luminescent magnetic nanoparticles encapsulated in MOFs for highly selective and sensitive detection of ClO-/SCN- and anti-counterfeiting, Nanoscale 10 (2018) 8667-8676.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emission fingerprint relationships of low-level water in organic solvents based on ln3+-β-diketonate complexes in laponite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Li, Z. Li, H. Li, Emission fingerprint relationships of low-level water in organic solvents based on ln3+-β-diketonate complexes in laponite, Adv. Opt. Mater. 4 (2015) 156-161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective visualization of the endogenous peroxynitrite in an inflamed mouse model by a mitochondria-targetable two-photon ratiometric fluorescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Cheng, Y. Pan, L. Wang, Z. Zeng, L. Yuan, X. Zhang, Y.T. Chang, Selective visualization of the endogenous peroxynitrite in an inflamed mouse model by a mitochondria-targetable two-photon ratiometric fluorescent probe, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 139 (2017) 285-292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for hypochlorous acid and its biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Liu, Q. Wang, X. Jiao, H. Yao, S. He, L. Zhao, X. Zeng, A ratiometric fluorescent probe for hypochlorous acid and its biological applications, Dyes Pigm. 160 (2019) 989-994.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide-functionalized metal-organic framework hybrid systems to create multiple luminescent centers for chemical sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yan, Lanthanide-functionalized metal-organic framework hybrid systems to create multiple luminescent centers for chemical sensing, Acc. Chem. Res. 50 (2017) 2789-2798.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in the development of luminescent lanthanide-based supramolecular polymers and soft materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Kotova, S.J. Bradberry, A.J. Savyasachi, T. Gunnlaugsson, Recent advances in the development of luminescent lanthanide-based supramolecular polymers and soft materials, Dalton T. 47 (2018) 16377-16387.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanothermometers based on lanthanide incorporated periodic mesoporous organosilica

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Kaczmarek, R. Van Deun, P. Van Der Voort, Nanothermometers based on lanthanide incorporated periodic mesoporous organosilica, J. Mater. Chem. C 7 (2019) 4222-4229.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red-emitting fluorescent probe for detecting hypochlorite acid in vitro and in vivo

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A stable lanthanide-functionalized nanoscale metal-organic framework as a fluorescent probe for pH

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, K. Jiang, H. He, D. Yue, D. Zhao, Y. Cui, A stable lanthanide-functionalized nanoscale metal-organic framework as a fluorescent probe for pH, Sensors Actuat. B-Chem. 254 (2018) 1069-1077.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide based dual-emission fluorescent probe for detection of mercury (II) in milk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Tan, Q. Li, C. Ma, Y. Song, F. Xu, S. Chen, Lanthanide based dual-emission fluorescent probe for detection of mercury (II) in milk, Biosens. Bioelectron. 63 (2015) 566-571.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A pyrazole-containing hydrazone for fluorescent imaging of Al3+ in lysosomes and its resultant Al3+ complex as a sensor for F−
